three_body.instability_note: Moderate conflict between the three signals (30-pt spread). ESG score reads better than FTSE 100 position implies. The FTSE 100's overweight in energy and mining inflates relative ESG rankings.
three_body.resolution: Our resolution: Integrity 59/100 × WEM 31/100 → 'Engagement Priority'. Jangani, Date & Tucker (SSRN 5618192, 2026) show driver rankings shift across regime windows (COVID → COP26 → energy crisis). We anchor to verifiable harm metrics (emissions, pay ratios, enforcement records) that are regime-stable — not ESG labels that oscillate with market narrative.

summary_investor: Shell plc is projected to hold steady in its energy peer ranking (4→4 of 5) over 3 months. FTSE 100 position: 17th→18th percentile. WEM 31/100 — material ESG risk — recommend underweight or significant risk premium. Placebo Index 0.79 — ESG narrative overstates real performance.
summary_corporate: 12-month sector ranking forecast: hold steady (4→4 of 5). FTSE 100: 17th→19th percentile. Highest-leverage action: Ecological Impacts (64% materiality) — this single driver has the greatest impact on FTSE 100 ranking improvement. ESG scores overstate performance by 16 pts — close this gap by improving Ecological Impacts disclosure quality.
summary_auditor: Of 2 ESG claims analysed, 0 (0%) are independently verifiable. 1 claim(s) directly contradicted by external data — moderate restatement risk. ESG provider spread: 10 pts — within normal range. Priority: third-party audit of Ecological Impacts measurement methodology.
crowd_consensus: Aggregated signal across investor, corporate, and audit lenses: Lagging — underweight; high harm + ESG overstatement confirmed. Driver rankings are temporally unstable across market regimes (Jangani et al., SSRN 5618192). The 3-body instability — ESG score, peer rank, FTSE position pulling in different directions — is resolved by WEM (31/100) × Integrity (59/100): regime-stable signals grounded in verifiable harm, not rater methodology.
